symptom: Connected Registry Arc extension creation stuck in state 'Running' - PVC phase stays in 'pending'
rootCause: Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) cannot bind - either the desired storage class is not available in the cluster or insufficient storage space
solution: 1) Check PVC status: kubectl get pvc -n connected-registry -o yaml connected-registry-pvc. 2) If storageclass missing: recreate extension with --config pvc.storageClassName='standard'. 3) If insufficient space: recreate with --config pvc.storageRequest='250Gi'
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Connected Registry

symptom: Connected Registry Arc extension creation stuck in 'Running' - logs show UNAUTHORIZED error with 'Incorrect Password'
rootCause: The connected registry connection string in protected-settings-extension.json is invalid or expired
solution: 1) Regenerate protected-settings-extension.json with: az acr connected-registry get-settings --name <name> --registry <acr> --parent-protocol https --generate-password 1 --query ACR_REGISTRY_CONNECTION_STRING --output tsv --yes. 2) Update extension: az k8s-extension update --cluster-name $ARC_NAME --cluster-type connectedClusters --name $ARC_NAME -g <rg> --config-protected-file protected-settings-extension.json
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Connected Registry

symptom: Connected Registry extension created but status is not 'Online' - logs show ALREADY_ACTIVATED error: 'Failed to activate the connected registry as it is already activated by another instance'
rootCause: A previous Connected Registry extension was deleted but the connected registry resource was not deactivated, so it still holds an active activation lock
solution: Run: az acr connected-registry deactivate -n <connected-registry-name> -r <acr-name>. The pod will be recreated automatically within a few minutes and the error should disappear.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Connected Registry

symptom: Customer receives ACR API deprecation email notification for 2018-02-01-preview API but claims they are not using the deprecated API
rootCause: ARM PolicyScan service (clientApplicationId: 1d78a85d-813d-46f0-b496-dd72f50a3ec0) is invoking the deprecated API on the customer's subscription, not the customer themselves. Some subscriptions were incorrectly included in the notification list.
solution: Run Kusto query on armprodgbl cluster to verify userAgent: if only 'PolicyScan' appears with the known clientApplicationId, no customer action is needed. Query: cluster('armprodgbl.eastus').database('ARMProd').Unionizer('Requests','HttpIncomingRequests') | where TIMESTAMP > ago(30d) | where subscriptionId == '<sub>' | where targetResourceProvider == 'MICROSOFT.CONTAINERREGISTRY' | where apiVersion == '2018-02-01-preview' | summarize count() by userAgent, clientApplicationId
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/ACR API Deprecation Handling for CSS

symptom: ACR API calls fail after deprecation date for preview API versions (2016-06-27-preview, 2017-06-01-preview, 2018-02-01-preview, 2017-03-01)
rootCause: Customer is using deprecated ACR API versions that have reached end-of-support
solution: 1) Migrate to a newer ACR API version listed at https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/templates/microsoft.containerregistry/allversions. 2) If using SDKs, update to latest version at https://azure.github.io/azure-sdk/releases/latest/index.html?search=containerregistry. 3) If using az CLI, update to latest version. 4) Use Kusto query on armprodgbl to identify which clients invoke the old API: check userAgent field.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/ACR API Deprecation Handling for CSS

symptom: Docker pull from ACR fails (timeout/connection refused) when client runtime is behind a corporate firewall or ACR has vNET firewall enabled
rootCause: When ACR vNET firewall is enabled, docker pull traffic routes through a regional data proxy (region-acr-dp.azurecr.io) instead of direct blob URLs. If the firewall does not allow the data proxy FQDN, pull fails. For geo-replicated registries, each replicated region has its own data proxy that must also be allowed.
solution: 1) Allow ACR REST endpoint (myregistry.azurecr.io). 2) Allow data endpoint: registry_name.region.data.azurecr.io (dedicated data endpoint) or region-acr-dp.azurecr.io (data proxy). 3) For geo-replicated registries, allow data endpoints for ALL replicated regions. 4) If customer objects to allowing *.blob.core.windows.net, use the dedicated data endpoint instead. Reference: https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/container-registry/container-registry-firewall-access-rules
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/ACR Behind Firewall

symptom: Content Trust signing fails when using ACR admin account as signer - permission error during docker trust signer add or docker trust sign
rootCause: ACR admin account does not support signer permission for Content Trust. Admin credentials are designed for basic push/pull operations only, not for Notary signing delegation.
solution: Use a Service Principal with AcrImageSigner and AcrPush RBAC roles instead of admin account. Grant signer permission as per: https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/container-registry/container-registry-content-trust#grant-image-signing-permissions
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/ACR Content Trust Delegation Demo and FAQs

symptom: After docker trust sign, unsigned/untrusted image appears in ACR repository alongside the signed version. Customer confused why image can be pulled with DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST=0.
rootCause: By design: docker trust sign first pushes the image as an untrusted image to the registry, then signs it via the Notary service. The unsigned image layer and the signed trust metadata coexist.
solution: This is expected behavior. With DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST=1, the client pulls trust metadata from Notary → verifies → finds digest → pulls by digest (trusted). With DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST=0 or pulling by digest directly, trust verification is skipped. Consumers must set DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST=1 to enforce signed-only pulls.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/ACR Content Trust Delegation Demo and FAQs

symptom: Investigating ACR image deletion: auth_user_name shows unknown application ID not found in customer's tenant, and http_request_remoteaddr_ipv4 is a Microsoft-owned IP
rootCause: The delete/untag action was performed by an ACR Task or 'az acr run' command, which executes on a shared ACR agent using a special service principal (not the customer's identity)
solution: 1) Cross-verify in ARM Kusto: query HttpIncomingRequests filtered by subscriptionId and ACR name around the event timestamp. 2) Check targetUri field to identify the operation type: 'az acr run', 'az acr purge', or ACR task (ca# run ID). 3) Note: both purge and untag commands can be content of ACR tasks.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/ACR How to find user of manifest event

symptom: ACR tasks not being triggered or failing to complete with no actionable output
rootCause: ACR task run failures can be caused by various issues including build configuration errors, trigger misconfiguration, or platform issues - root cause must be determined from BuildHostTrace logs
solution: Run Kusto query on cluster('ACR').database('acrprod').BuildHostTrace filtered by Tag containing '<acr>.azurecr.io'. Check RunnerLogs tag for build errors (Message column) and RunResult tag for trigger type and run metadata (DataJson column). Use RunId to correlate with Azure portal task run view.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/Check ACR tasks and outputs

symptom: Cannot delete ACR repository or images after setting lock attributes (delete-enabled/write-enabled) - deletion fails even after apparently unlocking
rootCause: ACR locks exist at three independent levels: Repository, Image by Tag, and Image by Digest. When changing locks by Tag, the output shows the related digest but the digest-level lock is NOT automatically updated. All three levels must be individually unlocked.
solution: 1) Check current lock status at all levels: az acr repository show for repo, --image repo:tag for tag, --image repo@sha256:digest for digest. 2) Unlock all three levels independently using az acr repository update with --delete-enabled true --write-enabled true. 3) Use batch scripts to automate multi-level lock management across all images in a repository.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/Enable image and repository deletion with ACR locks

symptom: ACR returns 'too many requests' throttling error during image pull or push operations
rootCause: Customer exceeds the concurrent request limit for their ACR tier (e.g., 10 concurrent pulls for Standard). ACR throttles requests beyond the tier limit.
solution: 1) Verify throttling with Kusto: cluster('ACR').database('acrprod').RegistryActivity | where PreciseTimeStamp > ago(1d) | where err_code == 'toomanyrequests' | where http_request_host contains '<registry>.azurecr.io'. 2) If business requires more concurrent pulls, customer must upgrade to a higher ACR tier (Basic→Standard→Premium) which increases rate limits. 3) Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient throttling.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/How to Handle Throttling errors

symptom: Cannot pull images from Microsoft Container Registry (MCR) - connection timeouts or blocked requests when pulling microsoft/* images
rootCause: Client firewall or network security rules are blocking the required MCR endpoints. MCR uses two endpoints: REST endpoint (mcr.microsoft.com) for content discovery and data endpoint (*.data.mcr.microsoft.com) for content delivery.
solution: Add the following firewall rules: 1) HTTPS to mcr.microsoft.com (REST endpoint). 2) HTTPS to *.data.mcr.microsoft.com (data endpoint, replaced *.cdn.mscr.io since March 2020). 3) Remove legacy *.cdn.mscr.io rules if present. Reference: https://github.com/microsoft/containerregistry/blob/master/client-firewall-rules.md
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/How Tos/Microsoft Container Registry (MCR) Client Firewall Rules Configuration

symptom: Intermittent 503 'Egress is over the account limit' errors during ACR image pull, reported from terraform/helm or docker clients
rootCause: Internal Azure Storage account used by ACR is experiencing bandwidth throttling. The storage account is on an internal Azure subscription not accessible to the customer. The problem is in Azure Storage infrastructure, not ACR itself.
solution: 1) Verify origin with Kusto: RegistryActivity | where http_response_status == '307' | where http_request_host == '<registry>.azurecr.io' | summarize count() by bin(PreciseTimeStamp, 1d), azure_blobaccount_name. 2) For intermittent issues: implement retries with exponential backoff. 3) For prolonged/constant 503s: raise an ICM to investigate with Azure Storage support team.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/503 Egress is over the account limit

symptom: ACR authentication fails with 401/403 after disabling ARM-scoped authentication (azureADAuthenticationAsArmPolicy disabled) - affects az acr login, docker push/pull, CI/CD pipelines
rootCause: When ARM audience token authentication is disabled on ACR, the registry rejects ARM-scoped Microsoft Entra tokens. Default az login issues ARM-scoped tokens, not ACR-scoped tokens. This is working-as-designed behavior, not a network issue.
solution: 1) Update auth flow to request ACR-scoped token: az login --scope https://containerregistry.azure.net/.default. 2) Then: az acr login -n <registry>. 3) Verify config: az acr config authentication-as-arm show -r <registry>. 4) To re-enable ARM auth: az acr config authentication-as-arm update -r <registry> --status enabled
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Authentication disable authentication as arm

symptom: ACR registry creation fails with 400 error due to DNS name conflict - CNAME already exists
rootCause: RP leaks CNAME records when registry creation fails during vNET handshake with NRP. The DNS name remains claimed even though the registry was never successfully created.
solution: 1) Verify via Kusto: RPActivity | where LoginServerName == registry | project env_time, CorrelationId, Level, HttpMethod, HttpStatus. 2) Use Geneva action Clean up orphan registry CNAME record to manually clean up. 3) Retry registry creation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR DNS And Name Reservation

symptom: ACR registry name not available after deletion - CheckNameAvailability returns NameNotAvailable_NotAllowedByPolicy when recreating in different tenant
rootCause: Name Reservation Service (NRS) reserves deleted registry names: Internal=infinite, Enterprise/CSP=180 days, others=30 days to prevent DNS sniping.
solution: 1) Verify NRS reservation via Kusto: RPActivity | where TaskName == NameReservationServiceManagement | where Message contains name. 2) If reason is NameNotAvailable_NotAllowedByPolicy -> ACIS action Force Delete Registry name from NRS with original sub/tenant IDs. 3) Wait up to 1 hour. 4) If ACIS fails -> submit ICM to Azure DNS Name Reservation team.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR DNS And Name Reservation

symptom: Docker push from Docker Desktop fails with UNAUTHORIZED error - all token server requests return 401 with basic_credentials_invalid
rootCause: Docker Desktop on Windows uses Windows Credential Manager (wincred). Stale or corrupt credentials cause Docker client to fail authentication, sending requests without proper Authorization header.
solution: 1) Open Windows Credential Manager. 2) Navigate to Windows Credentials. 3) Find and delete stale ACR entry. 4) Retry docker login and push.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Docker push from Docker desktop failing with unauthorized error

symptom: ACR image pull fails with no route to host error when private endpoint is enabled - resolves to correct private IP but cannot reach it
rootCause: Private endpoint connection to ACR is in Disconnected state - removed by the private-link resource owner. Effective routes on AKS node NIC show incorrect routing to private endpoint IP.
solution: 1) Check NIC Effective Routes for private endpoint IP. 2) Verify PE connection status in Portal -> ACR -> Networking -> Private endpoint connections. 3) If Disconnected -> recreate private endpoint. 4) Engage network team if routing issues persist.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR FailedPullImage NoRouteToHost PE

symptom: Customer accidentally deleted images or repositories from ACR and needs recovery - soft delete not enabled or retention period (7 days) exceeded
rootCause: Images were deleted from ACR. Soft delete default retention is 7 days. Recovery requires PG/TA assistance via Kusto to find deleted artifacts.
solution: 1) Query deleted repos via Kusto RegistryManifestEvent where Action==DeleteRepository. 2) Get tags from WorkerServiceActivity. 3) Confirm tag/digest with customer. 4) Reach out to TA for image recovery.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Image or Repository Recovery

symptom: Customer needs more than 200 private endpoints for ACR - default limit reached
rootCause: ACR default private endpoint limit is 200 per registry (Premier SKU), by design for ARM and geo-replication RP access consistency.
solution: 1) Communicate limitations: no geo-replication changes after increase; registry count <10 per region per sub; Premier SKU required; new limit 201-1000. 2) Verify registry count via Kusto RegistryMasterData. 3) Create ICM Sev3 via ASC to ACR PG. 4) Confirm: az acr show-usage --name <registry>.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Increase private endpoint limit

symptom: Customer cannot perform ACR operations despite existing role assignments - authorization fails after ABAC repository permissions (EnableRepoPermission) is enabled
rootCause: When registry roleAssignmentMode changes to AbacRepositoryPermissions, a different set of built-in roles is recognized. Legacy role assignments no longer grant expected permissions.
solution: 1) Check EnableRepoPermission via Kusto RegistryMasterData or ARM property roleAssignmentMode. 2) If True/AbacRepositoryPermissions -> use ABAC-compatible roles from MS Learn ACR role directory reference. 3) Customer self-diagnose: az acr check-health -n <registry> --repository <repo>.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Authorization RBAC-ABAC

symptom: AKS image pull fails with 401 Unauthorized - failed to fetch anonymous token but actual issue is image not found (404) - confusing error message
rootCause: AKS kubelet sends two parallel requests: anonymous (empty creds) and credentialed. For non-existing images, credentialed returns 404 while anonymous returns 401. This AKS-specific retry logic produces misleading 401 alongside real 404.
solution: 1) Verify image exists: az acr repository show-tags --name <registry> --repository <repo>. 2) The 401 is a red herring from AKS kubelet retry logic. 3) Focus on the 404 not found portion. 4) Correct image tag/name in deployment manifest.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Pull Image 401 Anonymous Token

symptom: Docker/Helm push to ACR succeeds without error but pushed image has no content - content is missing from repository
rootCause: Azure quarantine feature blocks content from being pushed. Quarantine is auto-enabled when Defender for Cloud is enabled on the registry.
solution: 1) Get ACR resource ID: id=$(az acr show --name <registry> --query id -o tsv). 2) Disable quarantine: az resource update --ids $id --set properties.policies.quarantinePolicy.status=disabled. 3) Retry push.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Push Failure no content

symptom: ACR replication stuck in creating or deleting state — delete/create operations never complete
rootCause: Backend permission issue: a required role assignment for the ACR first-party application was missing on the customer's trust-managed subscription, causing the RP to fail the replication CRUD operation internally
solution: 1) Check ARM incoming requests (armprodgbl) to find the delete/create correlationId. 2) Cross-reference with ACR RP logs: cluster('ACR').database('acrprod').RPActivity | where CorrelationId == '<clientRequestId>'. 3) If error indicates backend permission issue, escalate to ACR PG oncall via ICM (serviceId 22003) or AVA escalation in acr-sup Teams channel to add the missing role assignment.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Replication CRUD TSG

symptom: ACR push or pull fails with 'unknown: The operation is disallowed on this registry' after reaching 40TiB storage limit
rootCause: The container registry has reached its 40 TiB per-registry storage limit; operations are blocked once this threshold is hit
solution: 1) Verify storage usage: az acr show-usage -r {registryName}. 2) Advise customer to set up retention policy, auto-purge, and delete unused images. 3) If increase needed: open ICM to ACR PG with subscription ID, registry name, and requested size. 4) Internal limit is 60TB max; new geo-replications not supported above 60TB. 5) SLA is not guaranteed above 40TiB. 6) Storage limit cannot be reduced once increased.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Storage Limit Increase

symptom: Intermittent push failures on geo-replicated ACR when storage is near the limit — one replica is full while the other is not yet synced
rootCause: Geo-replication sync delay: when one region's replica reaches the storage limit before the other has completed syncing, pushes to the full replica fail while the other still accepts; eventually both fail once sync completes
solution: 1) Check storage usage per region. 2) Implement purge/retention to free space below 40TiB. 3) If storage increase is needed, note that registries above 60TB cannot create new geo-replications. 4) Existing geo-replications continue to work above 40TiB but no SLA.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Storage Limit Increase

symptom: ACR Tasks fail with 403 Forbidden errors after June 2025 — tasks that previously worked with network bypass are now blocked
rootCause: Default behavior change in ACR: the new networkRuleBypassAllowedForTasks flag defaults to 'deny' when not explicitly set (Phase 2 of security fix for Tasks network isolation bypass vulnerability ICM#561798833). Registries that relied on legacy network bypass without explicitly enabling the new flag are affected.
solution: 1) Explicitly enable the new flag: az acr update --name <registry-name> --set networkRuleBypassAllowedForTasks=true. 2) Alternatively, use ACR Agent Pool (NOT available in 21V/Mooncake): az acr agentpool create --name <pool> --registry <reg> --vnet <vnet>. 3) Or build/push images from self-hosted environments with direct ACR access, bypassing ACR Tasks entirely. 4) For CSS: if customer needs DRI opt-in, contact ACR PG via Teams channel or IcM template.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Tasks Network Bypass Policy

symptom: Customer needs to purge/clean up ACR images but cannot use ACR Tasks due to network restrictions or does not want to enable network bypass
rootCause: ACR purge runs as an ACR Task by default, which requires network bypass if the registry has network rules configured; customers in restricted environments or who opted out of network bypass cannot use the built-in purge task
solution: Download ACR CLI binary from https://github.com/azure/acr-cli and run purge commands locally on own machine. This avoids dependency on ACR Tasks and network bypass — all operations occur within the customer's trusted environment.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Tasks Network Bypass Policy

symptom: ACR Tasks fail with error: 'ACR Tasks requests for the registry {registryName} are not supported since the subscription {subscriptionId} is using the free Azure credit payment plan'
rootCause: ACR temporarily paused ACR Tasks runs from subscriptions using Azure trial/free credits (including $200 USD free credits, Azure for Students, etc.) due to credit reorganization
solution: 1) Advise customer to upgrade to a paid subscription. 2) If customer needs access with free credits: collect info (what workloads, expected usage/load), then create ICM to ACR PG requesting access grant. 3) PG will review and may approve — confirm access and close case once granted. 4) No current ETA for lifting the pause for free trials.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Tasks Temporary Pause

symptom: az acr login fails with 403 CONNECTIVITY_REFRESH_TOKEN_ERROR when connecting via Private Endpoint in a Virtual WAN Hub architecture — traffic is incorrectly routed through Azure Firewall which resolves ACR FQDN to public IP
rootCause: In Virtual WAN Hub architecture, Azure Firewall in the Virtual Hub intercepts HTTPS traffic as a proxy and performs its own DNS resolution. Since Private DNS Zones cannot be linked to a Virtual Hub, Azure Firewall resolves ACR FQDN to public IP instead of private IP, causing ACR to reject the request from the public path.
solution: Create a new private endpoint for ACR in the same VNet as the source VM (bypass the Virtual Hub Azure Firewall). Update the Private DNS Zone to point to this new dedicated private endpoint so traffic stays entirely within the private network.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Unauthorized Access Errors with Virtual WAN Hub

symptom: Unable to add VNET/Subnet from a different subscription to ACR firewall configuration — the newly added VNET/Subnet disappears after page refresh with no error message
rootCause: The Microsoft.ContainerRegistry resource provider is not registered in the second subscription where the VNET/Subnet resides. Without RP registration, the cross-subscription VNET rule cannot be persisted.
solution: Register Microsoft.ContainerRegistry RP in the second subscription: az login → az account set -s <sub_id> → az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerRegistry. Then re-add the VNET/Subnet via Portal or CLI and save.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Virtual Network & Firewall Configuration Cross Subscription

symptom: ACR webhook fails to push to App Service with 500 InternalServerError — webhook configured across different subscriptions or targeting ILB ASE endpoint
rootCause: ACR webhooks under spoke model with cross-subscription configuration are not supported. The Kudu site (webhook endpoint) must be publicly accessible because ACR does not support making outbound calls through a VNET to Kudu in ILB ASE environments.
solution: Use Azure DevOps pipelines instead of ACR webhooks for CI/CD to App Service in ILB ASE. If ACR cannot reach the VNet where ASE resides, webhooks will not work. Ensure webhook target and ACR are in the same subscription, or switch to pipeline-based deployment.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Webhook fails to push to App Service

symptom: AKS image pull fails with 'unauthorized: authentication required' when cluster uses Managed Identity — no imagePullSecrets configured in pod YAML
rootCause: The Kubelet managed identity does not have AcrPull role assignment on the ACR. When ACR is attached to AKS, AcrPull should be auto-assigned to Kubelet MI, but if the assignment is missing or the user performing attach lacked Owner/UAA permissions, the role was not created.
solution: Option 1: Detach and reattach ACR (user must have Owner or Contributor+UAA): az aks update --detach-acr <acr> then --attach-acr <acr>. Option 2: Manually find Kubelet MI via az aks show --query identityProfile.kubeletidentity.resourceId, then assign AcrPull role on ACR IAM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR authentication with AKS deployed using Managed Identity

symptom: ACR is reporting as disabled — cannot login, cannot pull images, re-enabling admin user shows ACR disabled state
rootCause: ARM maintenance during an update ACR request caused an inconsistency between the ARM resource state and the ACR resource provider state, leading the RP to set the registry as disabled. This blocks docker login since it requires credential retrieval from the service first.
solution: Open an ICM with ACR PG Triage team and request them to review the Master Entity for the affected registry. Use Kusto query on armprodgbl cluster to confirm if the subscription/ACR shows 404 during the timeframe, indicating ARM inconsistency.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR disabled issue

symptom: Customer reports image tags disappearing from ACR - suspected retention policy but Delete events in Kusto show Tag populated (WithTag > 0), indicating external automation not retention
rootCause: External CI/CD automation (pipeline/script/scheduled job) is deleting tagged images. ACR retention policy only deletes untagged manifests after configured days. Delete events with populated Tag field are NOT from retention. Similar delete counts across repos (e.g., 30/30) indicate rule-based cleanup (keep last N tags).
solution: Query RegistryManifestEvent in Kusto: 1) Check Delete vs PurgeManifest volumes; 2) Compare WithTag vs WithoutTag counts - WithTag>0 confirms automation not retention; 3) Check burst patterns (5m bins) to identify scheduled jobs; 4) Review deleted tag names for CI/CD patterns (PR IDs, build numbers). If mostly PurgeManifest with no tagged deletes → retention. If tagged deletes in bursts → customer automation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR image deletion investigation

symptom: AKS Service Connector job fails to pull SC operator image from ACR with 'not found' error - sc-extension remains in failed state, ACR only shows newer tags but extension keeps trying old removed tag (e.g., sc-operator:20250417.1)
rootCause: Service Connector (SC) extension does not auto-update. The installed version is hard-pinned to a specific image tag. When that tag is removed from ACR (scaksextension.azurecr.io), kubelet cannot pull the image and SC job fails repeatedly with NotFound.
solution: 1) Delete the outdated SC extension: az k8s-extension delete --name <extension-name> --cluster-name <aks> --resource-group <rg> --cluster-type managedClusters; 2) Recreate the Service Connection (this reinstalls SC extension with valid current tag): az aks connection create keyvault ...; 3) Verify sc-extension is healthy in Portal → Extensions + applications; 4) Confirm new tag exists: az acr repository show-tags --name scaksextension --repository prod/image/sc-operator
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR image pulls fail service connector extension not auto updating

symptom: Azure Function App deployment fails pulling image from private ACR with 'Pull Image Over VNet' enabled - errors show 'manifest invalid' and 'unknown blob' in ACI/Function App backend logs
rootCause: Known limitation: For Premium and Dedicated Linux Function Apps, pulling images over VNET only supports media type 'application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json'. OCI image index format (application/vnd.oci.image.index.v1+json) is NOT supported for VNET pull. If the image uses OCI format, the pull fails with manifest invalid/unknown blob.
solution: Ensure the image being pulled uses Docker v2 manifest format (application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json), not OCI image index. Rebuild/re-push the image with Docker v2 format if needed. This limitation is not yet publicly documented (work item: msazure/Antares#30809544). See also: AzureAppService wiki page 879995.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR image pulls fail unknown blob

symptom: ACR image pull fails with 403 Forbidden when AKS uses selected networks - despite AKS LB IPs whitelisted and ACRPull role assigned, Kusto logs show ACR is hit from AKS private IPs instead of public LB IPs
rootCause: Service Endpoint for Microsoft.ContainerRegistry created on AKS subnet forces traffic via Azure backbone (private IPs), conflicting with ACR selected-networks firewall that only allows public LB outbound IPs
solution: Remove the conflicting Service Endpoint for Microsoft.ContainerRegistry from the AKS subnet. Service Endpoints enable private IP communication which conflicts with ACR selected-networks public IP allowlist.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR imagepull forbidden serviceendpoint

symptom: ACR retention policy for untagged manifests does not delete certain untagged manifests despite policy being enabled with retention days set
rootCause: ACR retention policy does not support MediaType v1 manifests or OCI image indexes (application/vnd.oci.image.index.v1+json). Only MediaType v2 manifests are purged. az acr import pushes images with unsupported media type.
solution: 1) Verify manifest MediaType using Kusto query on RegistryManifestEvent. 2) Ensure Docker/BuildKit is up-to-date to push v2 manifests. 3) Avoid using az acr import for images that need retention policy as it uses unsupported OCI index media type. 4) Manually delete v1/OCI index manifests if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR retention policy for untagged manifests does not work as expected

symptom: Deleted ACR repositories still appear in Azure Portal with NAME_UNKNOWN error; CLI returns 'repository name not known to registry' when attempting to re-delete
rootCause: Azure Portal caching logic retains deleted repository links/information until cache expires. Additionally, image locks on the registry can silently block delete operations from succeeding.
solution: Wait for Azure Portal cache to refresh (usually minutes). Verify delete succeeded via Kusto query on ACR RegistryActivity table filtering DELETE method. If discrepancy persists between repository listings and actual registry contents, escalate to ACR PG to run Geneva Action to resync ARM metadata. Check for image locks via az acr repository show --repository <repo> and remove locks if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR unable to delete repository

symptom: Untagged images in ACR still appear as vulnerable in Microsoft Defender for Cloud reports under 'Azure registry container images should have vulnerabilities resolved' even after untagging; images not visible when listing tags but Defender continues to flag them
rootCause: Untagging an image only removes the tag reference — the underlying image manifest and layers remain in the registry. Microsoft Defender for Cloud scans all manifests (including untagged ones), not just tagged images, so vulnerability alerts persist.
solution: 1) List untagged manifests: az acr manifest list-metadata --registry <acr> --name <repo> --query "[?tags==null].digest" -o tsv. 2) Purge untagged images: az acr run --cmd 'acr purge --filter <repo>:.* --untagged --ago 1d' --registry <acr> /dev/null. 3) Enable retention policy to auto-delete untagged manifests. Defender will stop flagging after manifests are physically deleted.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR untagged images showing in defender

symptom: ACR webhook creation fails with 'You do not have access for this operation, please contact the owner of this registry' even when user has Owner role on the ACR
rootCause: Azure Portal uses ARM deployment to create webhooks, requiring Microsoft.Resources/deployments/* permissions at subscription level. ACR Owner role alone only covers ACR-scoped operations, not the ARM deployment calls the portal makes.
solution: Create a custom RBAC role with Microsoft.Resources/deployments/read, write, validate/action, operations/read, and subscriptions/resourceGroups/read permissions. Assign this role at subscription level to the user in addition to their ACR Owner role. Alternatively, assign built-in Contributor at subscription scope (broader but simpler).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR webhook creation permission issue

symptom: AKS nodes fail to pull images from ACR with image pull errors during pod creation; authentication failures visible in Kusto blackbox logs
rootCause: Service Principal credentials used by AKS have expired, or the Service Principal configured in AKS does not match the one granted access in ACR IAM. SP credential expiry is the most common cause.
solution: 1) Check SP expiry: az ad sp credential list --id <SP-ID>. 2) If expired, reset credentials: az ad sp credential reset --name <SP-ID> --password <same-password-from-azure.json> --years <N>. Important: SSH to node, cat /etc/kubernetes/azure.json to get current SP secret before resetting. 3) Verify SP match: az aks show -n <cluster> -g <rg> | grep client and compare with ACR IAM. 4) If SP mismatch, add the AKS SP to ACR IAM with AcrPull role. 5) Test with docker login <acr>.azurecr.io -u <SP> -p <secret>.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/AKS Nodes Failing To Pull Images From Azure Container Registry

symptom: AKS integrated with ACR via 'az aks update --attach-acr' fails to pull images with errors: 'failed to resolve reference: EOF', 'Forbidden' on data endpoint, or 'failed to fetch anonymous token: 401 Unauthorized'. az aks check-acr shows 'ACR rejected token exchange: Forbidden'
rootCause: AKS cluster is behind a proxy server or firewall that blocks outbound connections to ACR REST and data endpoints. Even with attach-acr integration, the kubelet nodes need direct network access to ACR endpoints.
solution: Allow ACR endpoints on proxy/firewall: 1) https://<acr>.azurecr.io (REST endpoint), 2) https://<acr>.<region>.data.azurecr.io (data endpoint), 3) https://*.blob.core.windows.net (blob storage). See https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/container-registry-firewall-access-rules
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/AKS is unable to pull ACR images

symptom: ACR cache rule creation fails with 'Resource names must contain alphanumeric characters and must be between 5 and 50 characters' error
rootCause: Cache rule name does not meet naming requirements: must be 5-50 characters, only letters and numbers, optionally separated by hyphens.
solution: Rename the cache rule to comply with naming requirements: 5-50 characters, alphanumeric only, hyphens allowed as separators. No special characters or spaces.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Caching

symptom: ACR cache rule creation fails with error about unsupported upstream or login server when trying to cache from registries other than Docker Hub or MCR
rootCause: ACR Caching (artifact cache) only supports a limited set of upstream registries. Attempting to cache from unsupported registries is rejected.
solution: Only pull from supported upstream registries. Check latest supported list at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/artifact-cache-overview#upstream-support. Docker.io and mcr.microsoft.com are always supported.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Caching

symptom: ACR cache rule with credential set fails with 'does not have secrets get permission on key vault' error when trying to authenticate to upstream registry
rootCause: The ACR system identity (Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries) was not granted 'secrets get' permission on the Azure Key Vault used by the credential set.
solution: Grant Key Vault access: az keyvault set-policy --name <kvName> --object-id <acrSystemIdentityOID> --secret-permissions get. See https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/assign-access-policy
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Caching

symptom: ACR cache rule creation fails with 'Quota exceeded for resource type cacheRules for the registry SKU Standard' error
rootCause: Cache rule limit reached. Maximum 1000 cache rules per registry (previously 50 during preview). Note: upgrading SKU does NOT increase the cache rule limit — the error message is misleading.
solution: Delete unneeded cache rules to free up quota. The limit is per-registry, not per-SKU. Upgrading SKU tier will not resolve this issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Caching

symptom: Azure Container Apps creation fails after 15-20 minutes with 'ContainerAppOperationError: Failed to provision revision - Operation expired' when using ACR with long registry name (>30 characters)
rootCause: Known bug in Container Apps platform that cannot handle ACR registry names exceeding ~30 characters. The operation times out silently without a descriptive error.
solution: Use an ACR with a shorter registry name (under 30 characters). If customer cannot rename, create a new ACR with a shorter name and migrate images. This was a known Container Apps bug (CASE#2205120050002279).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ContainersApps Creation failure due to ACR name too long

symptom: ACR Continuous Patching cssc-patch-image task fails at push-image step with cache rule error when repository uses PTC (pull-through cache) based rules
rootCause: CSSC (Continuous Patching) workflows are not supported on repositories that contain PTC (pull-through cache) based rules. The patch task cannot push patched images back to PTC-cached repositories.
solution: Switch to Artifact sync based cache rules, or run CSSC workflow on a different repository that does not contain PTC based rules.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Continuous Patching

symptom: ACR Continuous Patching cssc-patch-image task fails with 'image not found in MCR' error during patching step
rootCause: The Copa tooling image required for patching is not available in Microsoft Container Registry (MCR). Copa uses specific tooling images from MCR to perform OS-level vulnerability patching.
solution: Contact upstream team (azcu-publishing@microsoft.com) or raise a PR on GitHub (microsoft/mcr repo, example: PR#3914) to publish the missing tooling image to MCR.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Continuous Patching

symptom: Intermittent Docker pull failures from Docker Hub with EOF error on Cloudflare CDN URLs; pods enter CrashLoopBackOff after AKS cluster upgrade (upgrade is unrelated)
rootCause: Cloudflare CDN sometimes categorizes Docker image pull redirect requests as WebRepositoryAndStorage traffic. If the customer's internal firewall blocks this traffic category, image pulls fail intermittently.
solution: Modify internal firewall rules to allow WebRepositoryAndStorage traffic category, which includes Docker Hub redirect traffic through Cloudflare CDN. Restarting deployments provides temporary relief but the issue recurs until firewall rules are updated.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Docker pull issues intermittently

symptom: HTTP GET requests to ACR endpoints (/v2/_catalog, /v2/<repo>/tags/list) return UNAUTHORIZED even though Anonymous Pull is enabled on the registry; docker pull works fine without credentials
rootCause: Known limitation — ACR Anonymous Pull only supports the Docker registry protocol (docker pull/push). Raw HTTP REST API calls to catalog and tag listing endpoints are not covered by Anonymous Pull and still require authentication.
solution: This is a confirmed limitation by ACR PG. No workaround available currently. Customers needing unauthenticated REST API access must use authenticated calls with a token. PG tracking for potential future support.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/HTTP GET does not work though ACR Anonymous Pull is Enabled

symptom: Intermittent 502 Bad Gateway errors during ACR image pull/push operations; 'connection reset by peer' errors from blob storage; failures happen sporadically and succeed on retry
rootCause: During image pull, ACR sends 307 redirect to Azure Blob Storage for layer download. Azure Storage front-end nodes can occasionally enter a half-up/half-down state where the load balancer still routes traffic to degraded nodes. This causes intermittent connection resets. The issue is in Azure Storage infrastructure, not ACR.
solution: Retry the pull/push operation — since load balancer distributes across many front-end nodes, retries will likely hit healthy nodes. Azure Storage PG has optimized reboot logic to reduce incidents. For persistent issues, reference ICMs 234383246, 238929152, 230185890. Implement retry logic in CI/CD pipelines.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Intermittent 502 responses from ACR

symptom: ACR login fails with HTTP 400+ response on /v2/ endpoint when using admin credentials or service principal
rootCause: Admin credentials may be incorrect (wrong password from portal), or service principal credentials are invalid/expired. Login failures hit the /v2/ endpoint which is the Docker registry authentication entry point.
solution: 1) Check admin enabled via ACIS GetBasicRegistryDetails endpoint. 2) Query RegistryActivity for login failures: filter /v2/ with status >= 400. 3) Have customer verify password in portal. 4) If admin creds confirmed correct but still fails → create ICM to ACR PG. 5) If SP creds fail → issue is with the SP, not the registry (check expiry, permissions).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Kusto Query to look up Login issues

symptom: Microsoft Defender for Cloud fails to scan ACR images with error 'The container registry was not accessible, please verify access permissions and network access' even with trusted Microsoft services enabled
rootCause: ARM audience token authentication (authentication-as-arm) is disabled on the ACR. Defender for Cloud relies on ARM audience tokens to authenticate to ACR for vulnerability scanning. When this is disabled, Defender cannot access the registry regardless of trusted services configuration.
solution: Enable ARM audience token authentication: az acr config authentication-as-arm update -r <acr> --status enabled. If blocked by Azure Policy ('Container registries should disable authentication with ARM audience tokens'), either exempt the ACR from the policy or disable the policy assignment.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Microsoft Defender for cloud doesnt scan images in ACR

symptom: Notation sign/verify/list/inspect commands fail with 'POST oauth2/token: response status code 401: Unauthorized' against ACR
rootCause: The identity used for Notation operations has not authenticated to ACR or lacks the required ACR roles (AcrPull for verify/list, AcrPull+AcrPush for signing).
solution: Assign AcrPull role (and AcrPush for signing) to the identity. Login to ACR with 'az acr login', 'docker login', or 'notation login' before running notation commands. See Notation authentication docs at notaryproject.dev.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Notary Image Signing and Verification

symptom: Notation sign fails with 'describe-key command failed: Caller is not authorized to perform action on resource' when using Azure Key Vault plugin
rootCause: The signing identity does not have the required Key Vault permissions. Notation AKV plugin requires Key Vault Crypto User, Key Vault Secrets User, and Key Vault Certificate User roles (RBAC) or equivalent access policy.
solution: For RBAC: assign 'Key Vault Crypto User', 'Key Vault Secrets User', and 'Key Vault Certificate User' roles to the signing identity. For access policy: configure sign/get/list permissions for keys, get/list for secrets, and get/list for certificates.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Notary Image Signing and Verification

symptom: Notation verify fails with 'authenticity validation failed: signature is not produced by a trusted signer' when verifying signed container image
rootCause: The root CA certificate used for signing is not present in Notation's trust store, so Notation cannot validate the certificate chain of the signature.
solution: Add the root CA certificate to Notation trust store using 'notation cert add' command. Ensure the 'trustStores' field in the trust policy references the correct trust store containing the root CA certificate.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Notary Image Signing and Verification

symptom: Notation verify fails with 'artifact has no applicable trust policy. Trust policy applicability is determined by registryScopes'
rootCause: The trust policy's 'registryScopes' property does not include the repository being verified. Notation matches the image's repository path against registryScopes to find the applicable trust policy.
solution: Update 'registryScopes' in trust policy to include the target repository (e.g., 'wabbitregistry.azurecr.io/net-monitor'). Note: registry-level scope like 'wabbitregistry.azurecr.io' is NOT supported — must specify full repository path. Use '*' to match all registries/repositories. Import updated policy with 'notation policy import'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Notary Image Signing and Verification

symptom: Customer accidentally deleted ACR and needs to recover/undelete the registry and its images
rootCause: Customer-initiated deletion of Azure Container Registry. ACR supports recovery within a limited window via PG/TA-assisted restore process.
solution: Recovery steps: 1) Customer creates empty registry with SAME name, same resource group, same region, same subscription, and SAME SKU (Basic=1, Std=2, Premium=3). 2) New registry must be created WITHOUT geo-replication or availability zones. 3) Contact a Technical Advisor (TA) to perform the recovery via Jarvis/Geneva dashboard. 4) For Gov cloud registries: open ICM to ACR PG instead. IMPORTANT: No SLA for recovery — do not commit timeline to customer. Private endpoints/links will NOT be recovered — customer must recreate them. To find deleted registry SKU/region, query RegistryMasterData in Kusto.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Recovery of Azure Container Registry

symptom: AKS create or attach-acr fails with 'Could not create a role assignment for ACR. Are you an Owner on this subscription?' when using Service Principal for authentication; Authorization_RequestDenied error on Microsoft Graph API call
rootCause: The Service Principal used for AKS creation does not have Azure AD permissions to read other service principal objects from the directory. The az aks create command calls Microsoft Graph API to look up the SP for role assignment, which fails with 'Insufficient privileges'.
solution: Method 1: Grant Azure AD Graph API read permissions to the Service Principal so it can read directory objects. Method 2: Assign the 'Directory Readers' Azure AD built-in role to the Service Principal. Either method enables the SP to perform the Graph API lookup needed for ACR role assignment.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Role Assignment Error For Service Principal

symptom: Need to list and identify all images/manifests older than a specific date across thousands of repositories in ACR for cleanup purposes
rootCause: No built-in single command to list old images across all repositories. az acr manifest list-metadata only works per-repository, requiring iteration for large registries (e.g., 20k+ repos).
solution: Use shell script: 1) az acr repository list --name <acr> --output tsv > acr.txt, 2) Iterate repos with configurable batch sizes, 3) For each repo: az acr manifest list-metadata --name $repo --registry $acr --orderby time_asc --query "[?lastUpdateTime < '<date>'].[digest, lastUpdateTime]" -o tsv. Script supports pagination for large registries.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Shell script to list images older than a year from repos in ACR

symptom: Docker push to ACR fails with error 'manifest sha256:xxx was soft-deleted, and can be restored if needed' — cannot push image that has same digest as a soft-deleted manifest
rootCause: ACR Soft Delete feature prevents pushing an image with the exact same digest as a manifest currently in the Recycle Bin. This is a safeguard to prevent accidental overwrites.
solution: Restore the soft-deleted manifest first: az acr manifest restore -r <registry> -n <repo>:<tag>. After restoration, the image will be in the live repository and you can re-tag or push normally.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Soft Delete

symptom: ACR import fails with 'Import failed: A soft-deleted image with digest sha256:xxx already exists in repository. Kindly restore the soft-deleted image if needed'
rootCause: ACR import operation detects that the target repository has a soft-deleted manifest with the same digest. Import is blocked to prevent conflicting state between live and recycled images.
solution: Restore the soft-deleted image: az acr manifest restore -r <registry> -n <repo>:<tag>. After restoration, the import is no longer needed as the image is already in the live repository.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Soft Delete

symptom: ACR soft-delete restore fails with 'METADATA_CONFLICT: Tag latest already exists linked to a different digest, try force restore instead to overwrite it'
rootCause: The tag being restored from the Recycle Bin already exists in the live repository but is linked to a different image manifest/digest.
solution: Use force restore flag to overwrite the existing tag: az acr manifest restore -r <registry> -n <repo>:<tag> -d sha256:<digest> -f. This will replace the current tag in the live repository with the restored image from the recycle bin.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Soft Delete

symptom: ACR soft-delete commands fail with 'Error: repository not found' on geo-replicated registries
rootCause: ACR Soft Delete does NOT support registries with Geo-Replication enabled. Soft-delete operations fail silently or with repository not found errors on geo-replicated registries.
solution: Remove geo-replication from the registry to use soft-delete features. This is a known limitation — soft-delete and geo-replication are currently mutually exclusive.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/Soft Delete

symptom: Subset of users cannot list ACR images in Azure Portal with 'Error retrieving Image Names'; browser console shows CORS errors on ACR oauth2/exchange endpoint; CLI works fine for same users; only users with large Azure AD group memberships are affected
rootCause: Users with large numbers of Azure AD security group memberships generate OAuth2 bearer tokens exceeding 8192 bytes (ACR's maximum HTTP header size). The oversized token causes ACR to return HTTP 400 without CORS headers, which the browser blocks. This specifically affects users with high group count but below the overage threshold.
solution: This is a known limitation with ACR's header size limit. Workarounds: 1) Reduce the number of Azure AD group memberships for affected users. 2) Use CLI instead of Portal for ACR operations. 3) Request the user to be added to fewer groups or use conditional access to reduce token claims. PG tracking for potential fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/ACR CORS Policy Blocked Portal Issue

symptom: AKS with HTTP proxy configured fails to pull images from private ACR with 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized; image pull errors on both kubelet and crictl pull; private endpoint DNS resolves correctly but authentication fails through proxy
rootCause: When AKS cluster uses HTTP proxy for outbound traffic and ACR uses private endpoints, the proxy intercepts ACR authentication requests. The proxy may block or modify OAuth2 token exchange requests, causing authentication failures even though DNS resolution and network connectivity to private endpoints work correctly.
solution: Ensure ACR private endpoint FQDNs are in the proxy's noProxy/bypass list: 1) <registry>.azurecr.io, 2) <registry>.<region>.data.azurecr.io. These must bypass the proxy since they should route directly through the private endpoint. Verify with nslookup from within the node. See https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/http-proxy for proxy configuration details.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/AKS/TSG/Networking/Outbound Connectivity/Unable to connect to private ACR via proxy

symptom: Cannot connect to ACR registry or pull images. Errors: 'Could not connect to the registry login server', 'dial tcp <IP>:443: i/o timeout', or ImagePullBackOff in AKS pods.
rootCause: ACR has private endpoints enabled with public access disabled, and the client is not on a VNET that is connected to the private endpoint. DNS cannot resolve private endpoint records from the client's network.
solution: 1. Use Jarvis 'Get Registry Private Endpoints' action (JIT scope: ACRSupport, Access Level: PlatformServiceOperator) to get the NRP PE ID. 2. Find the private endpoint in ASC to identify which VNET it is connected to. 3. Verify client/AKS node is on the same VNET as the private endpoint. If not, the client needs to be on an approved VNET or public access must be enabled.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Platform and Tooling/Tools/Jarvis/ACR Jarvis Actions New

symptom: ACR pull/push fails with error 'client with IP is not allowed access. Refer https://aka.ms/acr/firewall' or 403 Forbidden. Docker login returns 'denied'.
rootCause: ACR 'Selected Networks' firewall is enabled and the client's public IP address is not in the IP allowlist configured on the registry.
solution: 1. Use Jarvis 'Get Registry Master Entity' action (same JIT as Private Endpoints) to view IP Rules on the ACR. 2. Check if client's public IP is listed in the 'IP rules' field of the output. 3. If not allowlisted, add the client IP to ACR network rules in the portal or via CLI.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Platform and Tooling/Tools/Jarvis/ACR Jarvis Actions New

symptom: ACR registry restore operation shows 'Failed to execute operation after 10800 seconds' but also shows 'Registry has been restored. Proceeding to do a size recalculation of the registry (non-critical)' in output.
rootCause: Timeout occurs in non-critical post-restore code (registry size recalculation), not in the critical restore path. The critical operations (Registry Master, SMap, Tombstone) completed successfully.
solution: Do NOT re-run the restore operation. If output contains 'Registry {loginServer} has been restored. Proceeding to do a size recalculation of the registry (non-critical)', the restore succeeded. For TB-size registries, images may take up to 24h or longer to fully appear. After recovery, if customer cannot view ACR in CLI/Portal, sync the resource state in ARM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Platform and Tooling/Tools/Jarvis/ACR Technical Advisors Actions

symptom: Customer cannot push new images to ACR registry. Push operations fail.
rootCause: ACR registry has reached the 20TB storage limit. All service tiers (Basic/Standard/Premium) have a 20TB maximum storage limit.
solution: Contact ACR PG team to increase the storage limit beyond 20TB (customer pays per-GB rate for additional storage). Check current limit via Kusto: 'cluster("ACR").database("acrprod").RegistryMasterData | where LoginServerName == "<registry>" | project SizeThresholdInTiB = (tolong(SizeThresholdInGiB)/1024)'. Or use ACIS 'Get Basic Registry Details' action to view the SizeThresholdInTiB and SizeInGiB fields.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Platform and Tooling/Tools/Jarvis/ACR Technical Advisors Actions

symptom: Intermittent HTTP 502 responses from ACR during image pull/push operations. Error: too many requests when doing >10 concurrent pulls.
rootCause: Azure Storage tenant reboots cause temporary redirect issues in the ACR backend. Also triggered by exceeding 10 concurrent pulls (ACR throttles with 'too many requests' / err_code=toomanyrequests).
solution: Retry the pull/push request. Azure Storage tenants reboot and redirect queries to valid up-time tenants. For throttling due to high concurrency, reduce concurrent pull rate. Confirm 502s with Kusto on RegistryActivity (err_message != 'too many requests', level != 'info', http_request_host contains registry name).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/Platform and Tooling/Tools/Kusto/ACR Kusto Queries

symptom: az acr build fails with "denied: client with IP X.X.X.X is not allowed access" when ACR has public network access disabled and pipeline runs on Azure DevOps self-hosted agents (even if agent is inside the same VNet as the ACR private endpoint)
rootCause: az acr build uses ACR Tasks infrastructure which runs on Azure-managed compute, not on the self-hosted DevOps agent. The agent only triggers the command; the actual build executes on ACR task compute that egresses via public Azure IP ranges, which is blocked when public network access is disabled.
solution: Option A (recommended, strict private-only): Create an ACR Dedicated Agent Pool injected into the VNet: az acr agentpool create --registry <acrName> --name <poolName> --tier S2 --subnet-id $subnetId. Then add --agent-pool <poolName> to az acr build. Option B (if dedicated pool unavailable in region): Allowlist AzureContainerRegistry service tag IPs in ACR firewall: az network list-service-tags --location <region> --query "values[?id == 'AzureContainerRegistry.<Region>'].properties.addressPrefixes[]" -o tsv, then add those IP ranges to the ACR network rules.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/az acr build with Private ACR using Azure DevOps SelfHosted Agents

symptom: I worked on a case where customer had some questions on a new implementation of Docker in swarm mode and container networking. The questions are diverse and advisory type, and as there is no single knowledge base which covers all this, hence the article. Questions on Base Image: windowsservercore image size, nanoserver ASP.NET 4.0 support, best practice containerizing ASP.NET 4.0 app, Docker image patching layers, image replication, host-image patching impact. Questions on Swarm: Docker Engine version for mixed Linux/Windows swarm, stack support in Windows swarm mode, overlay network driver NAT endpoint behavior.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#64116

symptom: az acr login fails with DOCKER_COMMAND_ERROR: Please verify if Docker client is installed and running
rootCause: az acr login calls docker login under the hood, requiring both Docker client and Docker daemon to be installed on the machine
solution: Install Docker Engine, or use az acr login -n <acr-name> --expose-token which does not require Docker daemon

symptom: az acr login fails in Azure Cloud Shell: This command requires running the docker daemon, which is not supported in Azure Cloud Shell
rootCause: Azure Cloud Shell only provides Docker CLI, not the Docker daemon required by az acr login
solution: Use az acr login -n <acr-name> --expose-token which works without Docker daemon, or run the command in an environment where Docker daemon is installed

symptom: Docker pull/login fails with unauthorized: authentication required when accessing ACR
rootCause: Authentication failed due to incorrect/expired credentials (admin user password regenerated, service principal secret expired, token scope map password expired or lost)
solution: 1) Admin user: verify credentials in Access keys blade. 2) Token/scope map: regenerate password if unsure. 3) Service principal: check az ad app credential list --id $SP_ID for expiry, create new secret if expired. 4) Verify correct RBAC role (AcrPull/AcrPush/Contributor/Owner)

symptom: ACR login fails with Unable to get admin user credentials and CONNECTIVITY_REFRESH_TOKEN_ERROR, Access to registry was denied Response code 401
rootCause: The identity (user or managed identity) used to authenticate does not have sufficient RBAC permissions on the ACR resource
solution: Assign the appropriate Azure built-in role (AcrPull, AcrPush, Contributor, Owner) to the identity. Run az login to refresh permissions. Verify subscription context is correct.

symptom: ACR login/pull fails with client with IP <ip-address> is not allowed access (403 DENIED)
rootCause: ACR built-in firewall is restricting public access - the client IP is not in the allowed list, or public network access is fully disabled
solution: Add the client IP to ACR firewall rules (Configure public IP network rules), or use Azure Private Link for private connectivity. Check Networking blade > Public access tab for current firewall config.

symptom: ACR FQDN resolves to public IP instead of private IP even though Private Link and private endpoint are configured - nslookup returns public IP despite canonical name showing privatelink.azurecr.io
rootCause: The private endpoint network interface is on a different VNet than the device performing DNS resolution, and the private DNS zone lacks a virtual network link to that VNet
solution: Add a virtual network link at the ACR private DNS zone level (<acr>.privatelink.azurecr.io) for the VNet where the querying device resides. Use Azure portal (Private DNS zones > Virtual network links > Add) or az network private-dns link vnet create.

symptom: ACR FQDN resolves to public IP when using custom DNS server - private endpoint configured but DNS resolution returns public IP
rootCause: Custom DNS server is not configured with a server-level forwarder to Azure DNS (168.63.129.16), so it cannot resolve the privatelink.azurecr.io records from the Azure private DNS zone
solution: Configure a server-level forwarder to Azure DNS (168.63.129.16) on the custom DNS server. The exact steps depend on the DNS platform (Windows Server DNS, CoreDNS, etc.)

symptom: ACR FQDN resolves to public IP - custom DNS has forwarder to Azure DNS but still returns public IP
rootCause: The custom DNS server VNet is not linked to the ACR private DNS zone, so Azure DNS cannot find the private A records when the forwarder query reaches it
solution: Add a virtual network link at the ACR private DNS zone for the VNet where the custom DNS server resides, using Azure portal or az network private-dns link vnet create

symptom: Cannot delete empty ACR repository - error: repository name not known to registry (NAME_UNKNOWN)
rootCause: Orphaned metadata left behind when images were previously deleted from the repository; the repository entry still exists but has no manifests
solution: Do not try to delete an empty repository directly. Push a dummy image to the repository first, then delete the entire repository (which deletes all images, tags, unique layers, and manifests).

symptom: Cannot delete Azure Container Registry that has private endpoints associated with it
rootCause: ACR deletion is blocked when private endpoint connections still exist on the registry resource
solution: Remove all private endpoint connections first using Azure portal or az acr private-endpoint-connection delete, then delete the ACR

symptom: ACR storage usage does not decrease after running acr purge to delete many images
rootCause: ACR uses layer deduplication - multiple manifests can reference the same layers. Deleting an image only removes its manifest and unique layers; shared layers remain because other manifests still reference them.
solution: This is by design. Storage reduction is less than expected when deleted images share layers with remaining images. To maximize space recovery, delete all images that share common base layers, or delete entire repositories.

symptom: ACR repository/image deletion fails with The operation is disallowed on this registry, repository or image
rootCause: A lock exists on the repository, manifest, or image tag - the changeableAttributes.writeEnabled is set to false, preventing delete operations
solution: 1) Check locks: az acr repository show --name <reg> --repository <repo>. 2) Check manifest locks: az acr manifest list-metadata --registry <reg> --name <repo>. 3) Check tag locks: az acr repository show --name <reg> --image <img:tag>. 4) Remove lock: az acr repository update --name <reg> --repository <repo> --write-enabled true (same for manifest/tag level).

symptom: Docker pull from ACR fails with context deadline exceeded error
rootCause: The device cannot resolve the ACR login server FQDN (<acr>.azurecr.io) via DNS - either DNS is misconfigured or the custom DNS server cannot reach Azure DNS
solution: 1) Run nslookup <acr>.azurecr.io to test DNS resolution. 2) Test with Azure DNS directly: nslookup <acr>.azurecr.io 168.63.129.16. 3) If Azure DNS resolves but device DNS does not, check custom DNS server configuration and forwarder settings.

symptom: Docker pull fails with I/O timeout: dial tcp <storage-ip>:443: i/o timeout - error pulling image configuration after multiple attempts
rootCause: Port 443 connectivity to the ACR storage/data endpoint is blocked. ACR allocates blob storage for image layers; the client must reach the storage endpoint (*.blob.core.windows.net or <acr>.<region>.data.azurecr.io if dedicated data endpoints enabled)
solution: 1) Test connectivity: telnet/nc to the data endpoint on port 443. 2) Allow the storage domain in firewall (prefer *.blob.core.windows.net or dedicated data endpoint FQDN over IP). 3) If using dedicated data endpoints, allow <acr>.<region>.data.azurecr.io. 4) Use Storage.<region> service tag in NSG rules.

symptom: Docker pull from ACR on Azure VM fails with I/O timeout - NSG blocking outbound traffic to storage endpoint
rootCause: Network Security Group (NSG) associated with the VM NIC or subnet is blocking outbound TCP 443 traffic to the ACR storage/data endpoint IP ranges
solution: 1) Use Network Watcher NSG Diagnostics to check if traffic to Storage.<region> IPs is denied. 2) Add outbound allow rule for port 443 to Storage service tag or specific storage IPs. 3) Verify route table does not drop traffic (next hop None). 4) If traffic routes through a firewall appliance, ensure it allows port 443 to storage endpoints.

symptom: Docker pull fails with manifest unknown: manifest tagged by <tag> is not found
rootCause: The specified image tag does not exist in the ACR repository - it may have been deleted, never pushed, or misspelled
solution: Verify the tag exists: 1) Azure portal: Container registries > Repositories > select repo > check tags list. 2) CLI: az acr repository show-tags --name <registry> --repository <repo>. Note: these commands only work if ACR network rules permit access.

symptom: Azure Web App fails to pull image from ACR with unauthorized error - admin user or managed identity authentication
rootCause: For admin user: incorrect login server/username/password configured in Web App environment variables. For managed identity: missing Microsoft.ContainerRegistry/registries/pull/read permission (AcrPull role), or the auto-assigned role was blocked by Azure Policy or deleted.
solution: Admin user: verify credentials in ACR Access keys blade match Web App environment variables. Managed identity: verify AcrPull role assignment exists on the managed identity, check Azure Policy is not blocking role assignment creation.

symptom: Azure Web App fails to pull from ACR with client IP not allowed - Web App outbound IP blocked by ACR firewall
rootCause: ACR firewall does not include Web App outbound IP addresses in the allow list, and public access is restricted
solution: Option 1: Add all Web App outbound IPs to ACR firewall (find in Web App Overview > Outbound IP addresses > Show More). Option 2: Configure VNet integration for Web App + ACR private endpoint - enable Pull image over VNet in Deployment Center and Container image pull in VNet config. Note: with public access disabled, ACR Tags dropdown may show Failed to load - manually enter image and tag.

symptom: ACR pull/push fails with request canceled while waiting for connection (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)
rootCause: Port 443 connectivity to the ACR login server (<acr>.azurecr.io) is blocked - possible causes include firewall, proxy, NSG, ISP restrictions, or route table dropping traffic
solution: 1) Test connectivity: telnet/nc <acr>.azurecr.io 443. 2) Check firewall/proxy/ACL/ISP restrictions. 3) For Azure VMs: use Network Watcher NSG Diagnostics to check outbound 443 to ACR login server IP. 4) Verify route table next hop is not None. 5) If traffic routes through a virtual appliance, ensure it allows port 443 to ACR.

symptom: ACR push fails with operation is disallowed - writeEnabled false or storage limit reached
rootCause: Either the repository/image/manifest has writeEnabled set to false (locked), or the ACR has reached its 40 TiB storage capacity limit
solution: 1) Check lock: az acr repository show --name <reg> --repository <repo> - if writeEnabled is false, unlock with az acr repository update --write-enabled true. 2) Check storage usage against the 40 TiB limit per SKU tier. 3) For image-level lock, also check az acr repository show --image <img:tag>.

symptom: Customer accidentally deleted Azure Container Registry or image manifests and wants to recover them
rootCause: Accidental deletion of ACR resources (registry or image manifests)
solution: 1) Customer recreates ACR in same subscription/resource group/name/SKU/Location with geo-replication OFF. 2) Collect deleted ACR resource ID, Location, Registry, and image manifest SHA (for image recovery). 3) Submit ICM to Azure Container Registry / Triage team (template: https://portal.microsofticm.com/imp/v3/incidents/create?tmpl=01n102). 4) After restore, validate registry with NEW credentials (auth info cannot be restored). Notes: No SLA for recovery; recovery available in Seattle time only; ~30 days retention, no guarantee; single image recovery recommended (whole repo is risky).
sourceRef: MCVKB/wiki_migration/======VM&SCIM======/18.15 _ MCVKB _ How to recover deleted Azure Conta.md

symptom: ACR Task fails to run when the registry has public access disabled (network-restricted ACR)
rootCause: ACR Task requires system-assigned managed identity and trusted service configuration when running against a network-restricted registry. Default authentication mode does not work with private endpoints.
solution: 1) Enable 'Allow trusted Microsoft services to access this container registry' on the ACR. 2) Create ACR task with --assign-identity (system-assigned MI). 3) Assign acrpush role to the system MI on the registry. 4) Add task credential with --use-identity [system]: az acr task credential add --name <task> --registry <acr> --login-server <acr>.azurecr.cn --use-identity [system]. For cross-ACR scenarios: base registry must also allow trusted services and task must use system MI for cross-registry auth.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/##New Feature/##Task.md

symptom: az acr build fails with error: 'client with IP x.x.x.x is not allowed access' even though 'Allow trusted services' is enabled on the ACR firewall
rootCause: ACR Firewall is enabled but the IP ranges for the ACR build service (AzureContainerRegistry service tag) are not in the allowlist. Known issue: https://github.com/Azure/acr/issues/627
solution: 1) Retrieve AzureContainerRegistry service tag IPs for your region: $serviceTags = Get-AzNetworkServiceTag -Location $region; ($serviceTags.Values | Where-Object {$_.Name -eq 'AzureContainerRegistry.$region'}).properties.AddressPrefixes. 2) Add retrieved IP ranges to ACR Firewall allowlist. 3) Retry az acr build.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/[ACR] Az acr build request blocked by Firewall set.md

symptom: ACR resources in China East 1 (CE1) and China North 1 (CN1) regions need migration due to region retirement
rootCause: CE1 and CN1 Azure regions are being retired; all resources in these regions must migrate to supported regions
solution: Follow Microsoft migration guide: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/management/relocation/relocation-container-registry. Note: AKS is NOT impacted (CE1/CN1 never supported AKS), but ACR can be created in CE1/CN1 so migration is required.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/Known issue or LSI/##ACR CE1 CN1 retirement.md

symptom: Need to restrict ACR repository access with fine-grained per-repository permissions instead of registry-level RBAC
rootCause: Registry-level RBAC (AcrPull/AcrPush) grants access to all repositories; finer control requires repository-scoped tokens
solution: 1) Create scope-map with per-repo permissions: az acr scope-map create (specify repos and permissions like content/read, content/write, content/delete, metadata/read, metadata/write). 2) Create or update token bound to the scope-map: az acr token create/update. 3) Verify in portal under Tokens section. Reference: https://docs.azure.cn/zh-cn/container-registry/container-registry-repository-scoped-permissions
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/##New Feature/##repository based token.md

symptom: Customer wants to use ACR Task dedicated agent pools in Mooncake (Azure China)
rootCause: Feature gap — ACR Task agent pools have not been deployed to Mooncake Cloud
solution: No workaround currently available. Inform customer this is a known Mooncake limitation. Track via ICM-570372647.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/##New Feature/##Task.md

symptom: Docker Content Trust (DCT) is being deprecated in ACR; new registries cannot enable DCT after 2026-05-31, full removal by 2028-03-31
rootCause: Microsoft retiring DCT feature in Azure Container Registry in favor of Notary Project
solution: 1) Migrate to Notary Project-based signing with Azure Key Vault: https://docs.azure.cn/zh-cn/container-registry/container-registry-tutorial-sign-build-push. 2) Review and update Azure Policy definitions that validate DCT to avoid deployment failures after 2026-05-31. 3) Existing registries with DCT enabled continue working until 2028-03-31; signatures and data permanently deleted after that date. Reference: https://docs.azure.cn/zh-cn/container-registry/container-registry-content-trust-deprecation
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/Known issue or LSI/## Docker 内容信任（DCT ）退役.md

symptom: Need to import container images from Azure Blob Storage into ACR (no direct docker push available)
rootCause: Images stored as tar archives in blob storage need a multi-step import process via ACR Task
