solution: Use ACR Task with multi-step YAML: 1) curl to download tar from blob (with SAS token or MI token). 2) docker load from tar. 3) docker tag to target ACR repo. 4) push to ACR. Two variants: a) SAS-based: use SAS URL directly in curl command. b) Managed Identity-based: create task with --assign-identity [system], grant Storage Blob Data Reader role to system MI on storage account, use 169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token to acquire token at runtime. Note: edit task.yaml on Linux (Windows line endings cause issues).
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/[ACR Task] import container image from storage to.md

symptom: Need to find the client/operator IP address that performed ACR push/pull/delete operations
rootCause: ACR logs the operator IP in RegistryActivity table but it may be PII-masked in Kusto; Jarvis retains the actual IP
solution: 1) Query Kusto RegistryActivity table filtering by registry host, time range, and repository name. 2) Find correlationid for the operation (look for manifest/tag actions). 3) Use correlationid to query Jarvis (Namespace: AcrRegistry, Table: RegistryActivity) — Jarvis shows the full IP while Kusto shows PII-masked values. 4) Filter for vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json or vnd.docker.distribution.tag media types to find the correct IP. Note: Kusto retains 90 days, Jarvis retains 30 days.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/[ACR]How to get the operator's IP.md

symptom: ACR RBAC custom role with wildcard (*) permissions fails with UNAUTHORIZED error on delete/push operations
rootCause: ACR does not support wildcard (*) permissions like Microsoft.ContainerRegistries/registries/*. ACR silently ignores wildcard entries, resulting in insufficient scope for bearer token authentication.
solution: Specify individual ACR data-plane permissions in custom role instead of wildcard: microsoft.containerregistry/registries/pull/read, push/write, artifacts/delete, sign/write, quarantine/read, quarantine/write, metadata/read, metadata/write, deleted/read, deleted/restore/action. Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/container-registry-roles?tabs=azure-cli
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/ACR/[ACR][RBAC custom role].md

symptom: AKS nodes lose access to MCR proxy after adding security appliance (e.g., Zscaler) — image pulls from mcr.microsoft.com fail
rootCause: Security appliances block outbound traffic to MCR proxy servers; the proxy IPs are not in the customer's allowlist
solution: 1) Ask customer to provide their outbound IP ranges. 2) Contact AKS PG (Andy Zhang) to whitelist the customer's outbound IPs on the MCR proxy. Process is simple — just email with the IP ranges.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/AKS/###Troubleshooting--MUST READ!!###/#Troubleshooting Guides/How to whitelist IP ranges on MCR proxy.md

symptom: Customer wants to use --aad-tenant-id option for ACR (like AKS) to point ACR to a different Azure AD tenant
rootCause: ACR is an Azure-native service that integrates natively with Azure AD and Azure RBAC; it can only authenticate against the AAD tenant where the ACR resource is hosted. Unlike AKS (which has an optional separate AAD integration for kubectl auth), ACR has no such option.
solution: 1) Use Azure Lighthouse to delegate resource management: designate a security group from the external tenant as delegated principal on the ACR resource group. 2) Alternatively, manage external users as guests in the ACR's home tenant. 3) For cross-tenant dev workflows: developers work with their own ACR, then a CI/CD process imports approved images to the production ACR. AKS can pull from ACR even when AKS users authenticate from a different AAD tenant — developers don't need direct ACR access.
sourceRef: Mooncake POD Support Notebook/POD/VMSCIM/4. Services/AKS/##Best Practice & FAQ/RE_ [--aad-tenant-id] option for az acr create.md
